infobox.value: is the third-largest [central business district](/wiki/Central_business_district "Central business district") in [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City") (after [Midtown](/wiki/Midtown_Manhattan "Midtown Manhattan") and [Lower Manhattan](/wiki/Lower_Manhattan "Lower Manhattan")[[2]](#cite_note-2)), and is located in the northwestern section of the [borough](/wiki/Boroughs_of_New_York_City "Boroughs of New York City") of [Brooklyn](/wiki/Brooklyn "Brooklyn"). The neighborhood is known for its office and residential buildings, such as [several of Brooklyn's tallest buildings](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings_in_Brooklyn "List of tallest buildings in Brooklyn") and the [MetroTech Center](/wiki/MetroTech_Center "MetroTech Center") office complex.

**Downtown Brooklyn** is the third-largest [central business district](/wiki/Central_business_district "Central business district") in [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City") (after [Midtown](/wiki/Midtown_Manhattan "Midtown Manhattan") and [Lower Manhattan](/wiki/Lower_Manhattan "Lower Manhattan")[[2]](#cite_note-2)), and is located in the northwestern section of the [borough](/wiki/Boroughs_of_New_York_City "Boroughs of New York City") of [Brooklyn](/wiki/Brooklyn "Brooklyn"). The neighborhood is known for its office and residential buildings, such as [several of Brooklyn's tallest buildings](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings_in_Brooklyn "List of tallest buildings in Brooklyn") and the [MetroTech Center](/wiki/MetroTech_Center "MetroTech Center") office complex.

Since the [rezoning](/wiki/Zoning_in_the_United_States "Zoning in the United States") of Downtown Brooklyn in 2004, the area has been undergoing a transformation, with $9 billion of private investment and $300 million in public improvements underway. The area is a growing hub for education. In 2017, [New York University](/wiki/New_York_University "New York University") announced that it would invest over $500 million to renovate and expand the [NYU Tandon School of Engineering](/wiki/New_York_University_Tandon_School_of_Engineering "New York University Tandon School of Engineering") and its surrounding Downtown Brooklyn-based campus.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

Downtown Brooklyn is part of [Brooklyn Community District 2](/wiki/Brooklyn_Community_Board_2 "Brooklyn Community Board 2") and its primary [ZIP Codes](/wiki/ZIP_Code "ZIP Code") are 11201 and 11217.[[1]](#cite_note-NYCPlanning-1) It is patrolled by the 84th and 88th Precincts of the [New York City Police Department](/wiki/New_York_City_Police_Department "New York City Police Department").[[4]](#cite_note-NYPD_84th_Precinct-4)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Downtown_Brooklyn&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

### Early development

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Downtown_Brooklyn&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Early development")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/10/Williamsburgh_Savings_Bank_Tower_by_DS.JPG/250px-Williamsburgh_Savings_Bank_Tower_by_DS.JPG)](/wiki/File:Williamsburgh_Savings_Bank_Tower_by_DS.JPG)

The [Williamsburgh Savings Bank Tower](/wiki/Williamsburgh_Savings_Bank_Tower "Williamsburgh Savings Bank Tower"), a prominent symbol of Downtown Brooklyn

This area was originally inhabited by [Lenape](/wiki/Lenape "Lenape") [Native Americans](/wiki/Native_Americans_in_the_United_States "Native Americans in the United States"), until the 17th century. The area close to the [Wallabout Bay](/wiki/Wallabout_Bay "Wallabout Bay") was called Rinnegokonk.[[5]](#cite_note-5) At that time the Dutch arrived, gained control of the land, and called it Breuckelen. The waterfront area being sold by Indians to [Joris Jansen Rapelje](/wiki/Joris_Jansen_Rapelje "Joris Jansen Rapelje"), who used the land for farm purposes. Until 1814, Downtown Brooklyn and [Brooklyn Heights](/wiki/Brooklyn_Heights,_Brooklyn "Brooklyn Heights, Brooklyn") remained sparsely populated. [Robert Fulton](/wiki/Robert_Fulton "Robert Fulton")'s new steam ferry then began to offer an easy [commuting](/wiki/Commuting "Commuting") option to and from downtown Manhattan. It made Brooklyn Heights Manhattan's first [suburb](/wiki/Suburb "Suburb"), and put Downtown Brooklyn on its way to becoming a commercial center, and the heart of the [City of Brooklyn](/wiki/History_of_Brooklyn "History of Brooklyn").

The city was home to many prominent [abolitionists](/wiki/Abolitionism_in_the_United_States "Abolitionism in the United States") at a time when most of New York was indifferent to slavery. Many Brooklyn churches agitated against legalized slavery in the 1850s and 1860s and some acted as safehouses as part of the [Underground Railroad](/wiki/Underground_Railroad "Underground Railroad") movement. [Walt Whitman](/wiki/Walt_Whitman "Walt Whitman") was fired from his job as a reporter at the Brooklyn Eagle due to his support for the [Wilmot Proviso](/wiki/Wilmot_Proviso "Wilmot Proviso") when he lived at Willoughby and [Myrtle Avenues](/wiki/Myrtle_Avenue "Myrtle Avenue"). A group of buildings at 223, 225, 227, 231, 233, and 235 Duffield Street, in addition to the African Wesleyan Methodist Episcopal Church located in MetroTech Center, were believed to be among the safehouses.[[6]](#cite_note-fny-6)

The middle 19th century growth of the Port of New York caused shipping to spill over into the City of Brooklyn; many buildings now used for other purposes were built as warehouses and factories. Manufacturing intensified with the building of the [Brooklyn](/wiki/Brooklyn_Bridge "Brooklyn Bridge") and [Manhattan Bridges](/wiki/Manhattan_Bridge "Manhattan Bridge"); buildings from that time include the 1915 [Sperry Gyroscope Company](/wiki/Sperry_Gyroscope_Company "Sperry Gyroscope Company") building, now known as the Howard Building of the [New York City College of Technology](/wiki/New_York_City_College_of_Technology "New York City College of Technology").[[7]](#cite_note-7) New, extensive infrastructure served the [Brooklyn Bridge trolleys](/wiki/Brooklyn_Bridge_trolleys "Brooklyn Bridge trolleys").

### 20th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Downtown_Brooklyn&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: 20th century")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fe/AdamsStreetRU.jpg/330px-AdamsStreetRU.jpg)](/wiki/File:AdamsStreetRU.jpg)

Adams Street/Brooklyn Bridge Boulevard, a major corridor through Downtown Brooklyn (2006)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fb/Downtown_Brooklyn_streetscape_April_2016_5.JPG/330px-Downtown_Brooklyn_streetscape_April_2016_5.JPG)](/wiki/File:Downtown_Brooklyn_streetscape_April_2016_5.JPG)

Tillary Street, another major corridor

Following [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), the [City Planning Commission](/wiki/New_York_City_Department_of_City_Planning "New York City Department of City Planning"), in conjunction with the Borough President's Office, presented and adopted a Master Plan for the Civic Center, which included an ambitious public improvements program. The program included plans for new buildings for City and State agencies, significant street widening and major housing construction in adjacent areas. A study conducted eight years later highlighted the progress made, emphasizing the widening of Adams Street (and later Boerum Place), which created a long and sweeping approach to Downtown Brooklyn from a modernized Brooklyn Bridge.

By the late 1960s, the patterns of transition that affected much of urban America initiated concern to protect the borough's Central Business District from deterioration. In 1969, a comprehensive plan for the entire city was completed and in the report the City Planning Commission stated, "Downtown Brooklyn's economy is vital to the borough and important to the entire metropolitan region."[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] In reaffirming Downtown Brooklyn's central role and identifying its problems, the Plan was optimistic that a combination of public and private efforts would stimulate office and commercial construction. A 23-story privately financed office tower at Boerum Place and Livingston Street opened in 1971 and the anticipated growth of the [Brooklyn Academy of Music](/wiki/Brooklyn_Academy_of_Music "Brooklyn Academy of Music") (BAM) succeeded far beyond expectations, giving this cultural institution an important role as a symbolic anchor amid increasing decay during the following decade.

After suffering with the rest of New York through the fiscal crisis of the mid-1970s, Borough President [Howard Golden](/wiki/Howard_Golden "Howard Golden"), first elected in 1977, moved forward with a more aggressive economic development program to revitalize Downtown Brooklyn. He identified the need for greater equity in resource allocation between Manhattan and the city's other boroughs. An important moment in the history of Downtown Brooklyn came in 1983 with the release of a Regional Plan Association report for the area. According to the document, Downtown Brooklyn could become the city's third-largest business district because of its proximity to Lower Manhattan (closer by subway than Midtown). It also could serve as a prime location for high technology industries and new market-rate housing. The [State Street Houses](/wiki/State_Street_Houses "State Street Houses") Historic District was listed on the [National Register of Historic Places](/wiki/National_Register_of_Historic_Places "National Register of Historic Places") in 1980.[[8]](#cite_note-nris-8)

### Rezoning

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Downtown_Brooklyn&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Rezoning")]

Historically, Downtown Brooklyn was primarily a commercial and civic center, with relatively little residential development. Housing included a few apartment buildings on Livingston Street, and seven 15-story buildings that make up the over 1,000 unit Concord Village co-op development on Adams Street, at the borders of both [Brooklyn Heights](/wiki/Brooklyn_Heights,_Brooklyn "Brooklyn Heights, Brooklyn") and [Dumbo](/wiki/Dumbo,_Brooklyn "Dumbo, Brooklyn").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/89/LawrenceStreet.jpg/330px-LawrenceStreet.jpg)](/wiki/File:LawrenceStreet.jpg)

Lawrence Street in Downtown Brooklyn as seen in 2006

Since the rezoning of parts of Downtown Brooklyn in 2004 to allow for denser residential development, the area has seen the arrival of new condominium towers, townhouses, and office conversions. In all, 14,000 residential units were planned for Downtown Brooklyn at that time.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] A New York Sun article from November 7, 2007, reports on the arrival of Downtown Brooklyn as a 24/7 community, estimating that 35,000 residents will come to the area in the next five years.[[9]](#cite_note-9) In January 2008, residents started moving into the new residential buildings, according to a New York Sun article.[[10]](#cite_note-10)
The [New York City Department of City Planning](/wiki/New_York_City_Department_of_City_Planning "New York City Department of City Planning") approved another, significant rezoning for portions of Downtown Brooklyn, including the [Fulton Mall](/wiki/Fulton_Mall "Fulton Mall") area, which resulted in significant expansion of office space and ground-floor retail, such as those at [City Point](/wiki/City_Point_(Brooklyn) "City Point (Brooklyn)").[[6]](#cite_note-fny-6) The rezoning consists of "zoning map and zoning text changes, new public open spaces, pedestrian and transit improvements, urban renewal, [and] street mappings".[[11]](#cite_note-11) The City Planning initiative also seeks to improve the connections between Downtown and the adjacent neighborhoods of [Cobble Hill](/wiki/Cobble_Hill,_Brooklyn "Cobble Hill, Brooklyn"), [Boerum Hill](/wiki/Boerum_Hill "Boerum Hill"), and [Fort Greene](/wiki/Fort_Greene,_Brooklyn "Fort Greene, Brooklyn").

As of March 2012[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Downtown_Brooklyn&action=edit), the rezoning of Downtown Brooklyn had caused gentrification in nearby neighborhoods.[[6]](#cite_note-fny-6) Affordable housing was created in the area after the 2004 rezoning, with 420 affordable units in 2014. The housing increase has also resulted in positive effects on other aspects of Downtown Brooklyn's economy as well, with revenues for the area's hospitality industry having tripled since 2004.[[12]](#cite_note-12) The increased residential development resulted in the growth of cultural institutions in the neighborhood as well.[[13]](#cite_note-13)

Some of this gentrification was controversial, however. In 2007, the city government was to acquire the houses at 223–235 Duffield Street via [eminent domain](/wiki/Eminent_domain "Eminent domain"), then demolish the houses and replace them with 500 new hotel rooms, 1,000 units of [mixed-income housing](/wiki/Mixed-income_housing "Mixed-income housing"), more than 500,000 square feet of retail space, and at least 125,000 square feet of new office space in the area; however, only 231 Duffield Street was replaced by a hotel.[[6]](#cite_note-fny-6) Still, this caused historians to protest over the planned demolition of the historic houses because of their importance to abolitionists during the [American Civil War](/wiki/American_Civil_War "American Civil War").[[14]](#cite_note-14)

## Institutions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Downtown_Brooklyn&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Institutions")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/da/Brooklyn_Boro_Hall_illuminated_2008_jeh.jpg/330px-Brooklyn_Boro_Hall_illuminated_2008_jeh.jpg)](/wiki/File:Brooklyn_Boro_Hall_illuminated_2008_jeh.jpg)

[Brooklyn Borough Hall](/wiki/Brooklyn_Borough_Hall "Brooklyn Borough Hall") with holiday lighting

Downtown Brooklyn is the civic and commercial [downtown](/wiki/Downtown "Downtown") center of the former [City of Brooklyn](/wiki/History_of_Brooklyn "History of Brooklyn"), which, as of 2020, has more than 2.7 million residents. Alongside immediately adjacent neighborhoods, the general area encompasses [Brooklyn Borough Hall](/wiki/Brooklyn_Borough_Hall "Brooklyn Borough Hall"), the [Justice Ruth Bader Ginsburg Municipal Building](/wiki/Brooklyn_Municipal_Building "Brooklyn Municipal Building"), the [Kings County](/wiki/Kings_County,_New_York "Kings County, New York") New York State Courthouse and the [Eastern District of New York](/wiki/Eastern_District_of_New_York "Eastern District of New York")'s [Theodore Roosevelt United States Courthouse](/wiki/Theodore_Roosevelt_United_States_Courthouse "Theodore Roosevelt United States Courthouse"). Attractions within the area include the [Fulton Mall](/wiki/Fulton_Mall "Fulton Mall"), the [Brooklyn Academy of Music](/wiki/Brooklyn_Academy_of_Music "Brooklyn Academy of Music"), the [New York Transit Museum](/wiki/New_York_Transit_Museum "New York Transit Museum"), and [Barclays Center](/wiki/Barclays_Center "Barclays Center").

Three days a week the Borough Hall [Greenmarket](/wiki/Greenmarket "Greenmarket"), featuring fresh produce from local farmers, operates on the plaza fronting Borough Hall. Formerly called Supreme Court Plaza, the location was renamed as Columbus Park in 1986.

### Points of interest

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Downtown_Brooklyn&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Points of interest")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/36/The_Brooklyn_Tower_010.jpg/250px-The_Brooklyn_Tower_010.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_Brooklyn_Tower_010.jpg)

The [Brooklyn Tower](/wiki/Brooklyn_Tower "Brooklyn Tower")

[MetroTech Center](/wiki/MetroTech_Center "MetroTech Center"), a business and educational center, lies between [Flatbush Avenue](/wiki/Flatbush_Avenue "Flatbush Avenue") and Jay Street, above the [Jay Street – MetroTech](/wiki/Jay_Street_%E2%80%93_MetroTech_(New_York_City_Subway) "Jay Street – MetroTech (New York City Subway)") subway station, north of the [Fulton Street Mall](/wiki/Fulton_Street_Mall "Fulton Street Mall"), and south of the busy Tillary Street.[[15]](#cite_note-15)[[16]](#cite_note-16)

The original location of [Junior's](/wiki/Junior%27s "Junior's"), which was founded by Harry Rosen in 1950,[[17]](#cite_note-rosen-dead-17) is located at the corner of DeKalb Avenue and Flatbush Avenue Extension. The restaurant is 17,000 square feet of red-and-white-striped menus, flashbulb-adorned signs, rust-colored booths, and a wooden bar. A shrine to the Brooklyn of old, it has become a must-visit for politicians from borough presidents to President [Barack Obama](/wiki/Barack_Obama "Barack Obama"), who bought two cheesecakes and a couple of black-and-white cookies during an October 2013 visit with [Bill de Blasio](/wiki/Bill_de_Blasio "Bill de Blasio").[[18]](#cite_note-18)

[9 DeKalb Avenue](/wiki/9_DeKalb_Avenue "9 DeKalb Avenue") is a residential skyscraper adjacent to the [Dime Savings Bank of New York](/wiki/Dime_Savings_Bank_of_New_York "Dime Savings Bank of New York"). Upon completion, it became the first supertall building in Brooklyn and the tallest structure in New York City outside of Manhattan.[[19]](#cite_note-realdeal2-19)[[20]](#cite_note-20)

[Cadman Plaza](/wiki/Cadman_Plaza "Cadman Plaza") Park, named for the historically prominent (and Brooklyn-based) [liberal Protestant](/wiki/Liberal_Protestant "Liberal Protestant") clergyman/broadcaster [S. Parkes Cadman](/wiki/S._Parkes_Cadman "S. Parkes Cadman"), provides 10 acres (40,000 m2) of green space in the neighborhood, and was recently renovated by the New York City Parks Department. These and other parks form a long mall from Borough Hall to Brooklyn Bridge. A new park is also planned for the area, known as the Willoughby Square Park.[[21]](#cite_note-21)[[22]](#cite_note-22)

## Bridge Plaza

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Downtown_Brooklyn&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Bridge Plaza")]

Main article: [Bridge Plaza, Brooklyn](/wiki/Bridge_Plaza,_Brooklyn "Bridge Plaza, Brooklyn")

At the northeastern corner of Downtown Brooklyn is Bridge Plaza, bounded by [Flatbush Avenue](/wiki/Flatbush_Avenue_(Brooklyn) "Flatbush Avenue (Brooklyn)") Extension and [Manhattan Bridge](/wiki/Manhattan_Bridge "Manhattan Bridge") on the west, Tillary Street on the south, and the [Brooklyn-Queens Expressway](/wiki/Brooklyn-Queens_Expressway "Brooklyn-Queens Expressway") (BQE) on the north and east.[[23]](#cite_note-23)[[24]](#cite_note-24) The newer term **RAMBO**, an [acronym](/wiki/Acronym_and_initialism "Acronym and initialism") for **Right Around The Manhattan Bridge Overpass**[[25]](#cite_note-25)[[26]](#cite_note-26)[[27]](#cite_note-27)[[28]](#cite_note-28) is sometimes applied to the area, comparing it to [DUMBO](/wiki/Dumbo,_Brooklyn "Dumbo, Brooklyn"). The neighborhood was connected to [Vinegar Hill](/wiki/Vinegar_Hill,_Brooklyn "Vinegar Hill, Brooklyn") until the 1950s, when construction of the BQE effectively isolated it from surrounding areas.

## Transportation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Downtown_Brooklyn&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Transportation")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/03/Jay_Street_%E2%80%93_MetroTech_station_entrance_via_AVA_DoBro_Building.jpg/330px-Jay_Street_%E2%80%93_MetroTech_station_entrance_via_AVA_DoBro_Building.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jay_Street_%E2%80%93_MetroTech_station_entrance_via_AVA_DoBro_Building.jpg)

Jay Street–MetroTech station entrance in the AVA DoBro Building

Downtown Brooklyn is connected with [Manhattan](/wiki/Manhattan "Manhattan") by the [Brooklyn](/wiki/Brooklyn_Bridge "Brooklyn Bridge") and [Manhattan](/wiki/Manhattan_Bridge "Manhattan Bridge") Bridges.

The neighborhood has extensive public transportation accessibility; it is served by the [New York City Subway](/wiki/New_York_City_Subway "New York City Subway") and many bus lines. All but one [Manhattan trunk line](/wiki/New_York_City_Subway_nomenclature#Current_status "New York City Subway nomenclature") in [Lower Manhattan](/wiki/Lower_Manhattan "Lower Manhattan") has a direct connection to Downtown Brooklyn. From south to north, the [IRT Lexington Avenue Line](/wiki/IRT_Lexington_Avenue_Line "IRT Lexington Avenue Line") ([4](/wiki/4_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "4 (New York City Subway service)") and ​[5](/wiki/5_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "5 (New York City Subway service)") trains) via the [Joralemon Street Tunnel](/wiki/Joralemon_Street_Tunnel "Joralemon Street Tunnel"), the [BMT Broadway](/wiki/BMT_Broadway_Line "BMT Broadway Line") and [BMT Nassau Street Lines](/wiki/BMT_Nassau_Street_Line "BMT Nassau Street Line") ([N](/wiki/N_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "N (New York City Subway service)"), [R](/wiki/R_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "R (New York City Subway service)"), and ​[W](/wiki/W_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "W (New York City Subway service)") trains) via the [Montague Street Tunnel](/wiki/Montague_Street_Tunnel "Montague Street Tunnel"), the [IRT Broadway–Seventh Avenue Line](/wiki/IRT_Broadway%E2%80%93Seventh_Avenue_Line "IRT Broadway–Seventh Avenue Line") ([2](/wiki/2_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "2 (New York City Subway service)") and ​[3](/wiki/3_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "3 (New York City Subway service)") trains) via the [Clark Street Tunnel](/wiki/Clark_Street_Tunnel "Clark Street Tunnel") and the [IND Eighth Avenue Line](/wiki/IND_Eighth_Avenue_Line "IND Eighth Avenue Line") ([A](/wiki/A_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "A (New York City Subway service)") and ​[C](/wiki/C_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "C (New York City Subway service)") trains) via the [Cranberry Street Tunnel](/wiki/Cranberry_Street_Tunnel "Cranberry Street Tunnel") provide that service. Slightly farther north, the [Manhattan Bridge](/wiki/Manhattan_Bridge "Manhattan Bridge") ([B](/wiki/B_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "B (New York City Subway service)"), ​[D](/wiki/D_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "D (New York City Subway service)")​, [N](/wiki/N_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "N (New York City Subway service)"), and ​[Q](/wiki/Q_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "Q (New York City Subway service)") trains) and [Rutgers Street Tunnel](/wiki/Rutgers_Street_Tunnel "Rutgers Street Tunnel") ([F](/wiki/F_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "F (New York City Subway service)") and [<F>](/wiki/Fd_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "Fd (New York City Subway service)")​ trains) also feed subway trains from the [Lower East Side](/wiki/Lower_East_Side "Lower East Side") into Downtown Brooklyn.

Major stations in the neighborhood are:

* [Jay Street–MetroTech](/wiki/Jay_Street%E2%80%93MetroTech_(New_York_City_Subway) "Jay Street–MetroTech (New York City Subway)") ([A](/wiki/A_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "A (New York City Subway service)"), ​[C](/wiki/C_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "C (New York City Subway service)")​, [F](/wiki/F_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "F (New York City Subway service)"), [<F>](/wiki/Fd_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "Fd (New York City Subway service)")​​, [N](/wiki/N_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "N (New York City Subway service)"), [R](/wiki/R_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "R (New York City Subway service)"), and ​[W](/wiki/W_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "W (New York City Subway service)") trains)
* [Court Street–Borough Hall](/wiki/Court_Street%E2%80%93Borough_Hall_(New_York_City_Subway) "Court Street–Borough Hall (New York City Subway)") ([2](/wiki/2_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "2 (New York City Subway service)"), ​[3](/wiki/3_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "3 (New York City Subway service)")​, [4](/wiki/4_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "4 (New York City Subway service)"), ​[5](/wiki/5_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "5 (New York City Subway service)")​, [N](/wiki/N_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "N (New York City Subway service)"), [R](/wiki/R_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "R (New York City Subway service)"), and ​[W](/wiki/W_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "W (New York City Subway service)") trains)
* [DeKalb Avenue](/wiki/DeKalb_Avenue_(BMT_Fourth_Avenue_Line) "DeKalb Avenue (BMT Fourth Avenue Line)") ([B](/wiki/B_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "B (New York City Subway service)"), ​[D](/wiki/D_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "D (New York City Subway service)"), ​[N](/wiki/N_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "N (New York City Subway service)"), ​[Q](/wiki/Q_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "Q (New York City Subway service)"), ​[R](/wiki/R_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "R (New York City Subway service)"), and ​[W](/wiki/W_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "W (New York City Subway service)") trains)
* [Hoyt–Schermerhorn Streets](/wiki/Hoyt%E2%80%93Schermerhorn_Streets_(New_York_City_Subway) "Hoyt–Schermerhorn Streets (New York City Subway)") ([A](/wiki/A_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "A (New York City Subway service)"), ​[C](/wiki/C_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "C (New York City Subway service)")​, and [G](/wiki/G_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "G (New York City Subway service)") trains)
* [Nevins Street](/wiki/Nevins_Street_(IRT_Eastern_Parkway_Line) "Nevins Street (IRT Eastern Parkway Line)") ([2](/wiki/2_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "2 (New York City Subway service)"), ​[3](/wiki/3_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "3 (New York City Subway service)"), ​[4](/wiki/4_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "4 (New York City Subway service)"), and ​[5](/wiki/5_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "5 (New York City Subway service)") trains)
* [Atlantic Avenue–Barclays Center](/wiki/Atlantic_Avenue%E2%80%93Barclays_Center_(New_York_City_Subway) "Atlantic Avenue–Barclays Center (New York City Subway)") ([2](/wiki/2_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "2 (New York City Subway service)"), ​[3](/wiki/3_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "3 (New York City Subway service)"), ​[4](/wiki/4_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "4 (New York City Subway service)"), ​[5](/wiki/5_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "5 (New York City Subway service)")​, [B](/wiki/B_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "B (New York City Subway service)"), ​[D](/wiki/D_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "D (New York City Subway service)"), [N](/wiki/N_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "N (New York City Subway service)"), ​[Q](/wiki/Q_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "Q (New York City Subway service)")​, [R](/wiki/R_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "R (New York City Subway service)") and ​[W](/wiki/W_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "W (New York City Subway service)")​ trains)

A $130 million capital project to connect [Lawrence Street–MetroTech](/wiki/Jay_Street%E2%80%93MetroTech_(BMT_Fourth_Avenue_Line) "Jay Street–MetroTech (BMT Fourth Avenue Line)") ([N](/wiki/N_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "N (New York City Subway service)"), [R](/wiki/R_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "R (New York City Subway service)"), and ​[W](/wiki/W_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "W (New York City Subway service)") trains) and [Jay Street–Borough Hall](/wiki/Jay_Street%E2%80%93MetroTech_(IND_Fulton_Street_Line) "Jay Street–MetroTech (IND Fulton Street Line)") ([A](/wiki/A_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "A (New York City Subway service)"), ​[C](/wiki/C_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "C (New York City Subway service)")​, and [F](/wiki/F_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "F (New York City Subway service)") and [<F>](/wiki/Fd_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "Fd (New York City Subway service)")​ trains), which also included renovation of both stations, was completed on December 10, 2010.[[31]](#cite_note-31) It features an underground corridor on Willoughby Street connecting both stations, which includes new escalator and elevator access to Lawrence Street.

The [Long Island Rail Road](/wiki/Long_Island_Rail_Road "Long Island Rail Road") stops at the [Atlantic Terminal](/wiki/Atlantic_Terminal "Atlantic Terminal"), located at the intersection of Atlantic and Flatbush Avenues.

## Education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Downtown_Brooklyn&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Education")]

Public schools are operated by the [New York City Department of Education](/wiki/New_York_City_Department_of_Education "New York City Department of Education").

In 2021 the private school [German School of Brooklyn](/wiki/German_School_of_Brooklyn "German School of Brooklyn") moved all levels to its permanent site at 9 Hanover Place in Downtown Brooklyn.[[32]](#cite_note-32) The [Khalil Gibran International Academy](/wiki/Khalil_Gibran_International_Academy "Khalil Gibran International Academy") High School opened in September 2024.[[33]](#cite_note-Russo-Lennon_2024-33)

Schools situated within or in the immediate periphery of the district include [Brooklyn Technical High School](/wiki/Brooklyn_Technical_High_School "Brooklyn Technical High School") (one of the city's nine selective [specialized high schools](/wiki/Specialized_high_schools_in_New_York_City "Specialized high schools in New York City")), [Brooklyn Friends School](/wiki/Brooklyn_Friends_School "Brooklyn Friends School"), [Bishop Loughlin Memorial High School](/wiki/Bishop_Loughlin_Memorial_High_School "Bishop Loughlin Memorial High School"), [St. Francis College](/wiki/St._Francis_College "St. Francis College"), [St. Joseph's College](/wiki/Saint_Joseph%27s_College,_New_York "Saint Joseph's College, New York"), [Brooklyn Law School](/wiki/Brooklyn_Law_School "Brooklyn Law School"), [New York University](/wiki/New_York_University "New York University")'s [Tandon School of Engineering](/wiki/New_York_University_Tandon_School_of_Engineering "New York University Tandon School of Engineering") and [Center for Urban Science and Progress](/wiki/Center_for_Urban_Science_and_Progress "Center for Urban Science and Progress"), the [New York City College of Technology](/wiki/New_York_City_College_of_Technology "New York City College of Technology"), [Adelphi University](/wiki/Adelphi_University "Adelphi University")'s Brooklyn Center, and [Long Island University](/wiki/Long_Island_University "Long Island University")'s [LIU Brooklyn](/wiki/LIU_Brooklyn "LIU Brooklyn") campus.
